 {"id":"process_triage-ica.1.2","title":"Implement rollback-safe self-update mechanism","description":"## Overview
Implement self-update mechanism with automatic rollback on failure.

## Background
Self-update can fail mid-flight leaving a corrupted binary. Rollback ensures users can recover to a working state.

## Scope

### 1. Backup Before Update
- Copy current binary to \`~/.cache/process_triage/rollback/pt-core-<version>\`
- Store metadata: version, timestamp, checksum
- Retain last 3 versions for rollback
- Cleanup old backups on successful update

### 2. Atomic Update
- Download to temp file first
- Verify checksum and signature before replace
- Use rename() for atomic replacement (same filesystem)
- Fallback: copy + unlink for cross-filesystem

### 3. Post-Update Verification
- Run \`pt-core --version\` after update
- Parse version output, compare to expected
- Verify basic functionality: \`pt-core health\`
- Timeout: 5 seconds for verification

### 4. Automatic Rollback
- Trigger: Version check fails OR health check fails
- Restore backup binary atomically
- Log rollback reason and details
- Exit with error code, clear message

### 5. Manual Rollback
- \`pt update --rollback\`: Restore previous version
- \`pt update --rollback <version>\`: Restore specific version
- \`pt update --list-backups\`: Show available versions
- Interactive confirmation before rollback

---

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/src/install/rollback_test.rs\`
- **Coverage target**: 95% for rollback logic
- Test cases:
  - Backup creation with correct metadata
  - Backup cleanup keeps last N versions
  - Atomic rename succeeds
  - Cross-filesystem copy fallback works
  - Version verification parsing
  - Rollback restoration correctness
  - Old backup pruning

### Integration Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/tests/rollback_integration.rs\`
- Test cases:
  - Full update + rollback cycle
  - Update failure triggers automatic rollback
  - Manual rollback to specific version
  - Rollback with corrupted backup (error handling)
  - Concurrent update protection

### E2E Tests
- **File**: \`test/rollback_e2e.bats\`
- Test scenarios:
  - \`pt update\` with valid new version succeeds
  - \`pt update\` with corrupted download triggers rollback
  - \`pt update --rollback\` restores previous version
  - \`pt update --list-backups\` shows available versions
  - Rollback works after simulated crash (kill -9 mid-update)
- **Artifact logging**: Backup paths, version changes, rollback events

### Crash Recovery Tests
- **File**: \`test/rollback_crash.bats\`
- Test scenarios:
  - Simulate crash during download: no change
  - Simulate crash during replace: rollback works
  - Simulate crash during verification: rollback works
  - Power loss simulation: recovery on next run

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`update.backup_start\` | INFO | current_version, backup_path | Backup begins |
| \`update.backup_complete\` | INFO | backup_path, size_bytes | Backup done |
| \`update.download_complete\` | INFO | new_version, size_bytes | Download done |
| \`update.verify_start\` | DEBUG | checks | Verification begins |
| \`update.verify_pass\` | INFO | version | Verification passed |
| \`update.verify_fail\` | ERROR | reason, expected, actual | Verification failed |
| \`update.rollback_start\` | WARN | reason, target_version | Rollback begins |
| \`update.rollback_complete\` | INFO | restored_version | Rollback done |
| \`update.cleanup\` | DEBUG | removed_backups | Old backups removed |

---

## Error Handling

### Failure Modes
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | User Message |
|----------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Download corrupt | Checksum mismatch | Abort, keep current | "Download corrupted. Update aborted." |
| Replace failed | rename() error | Keep temp, retry | "Could not replace binary. Retrying..." |
| New version broken | Health check fails | Auto rollback | "New version failed health check. Rolling back..." |
| No backup available | Backup missing | Error, no rollback | "No backup available for rollback." |
| Rollback failed | Restore error | Manual instructions | "Rollback failed. Manual recovery: INSTRUCTIONS" |

### Recovery Instructions
If all automatic recovery fails, provide clear manual steps:
1. Download known good version from GitHub releases
2. Replace binary manually: \`cp pt-core ~/.local/bin/\`
3. Verify: \`pt-core --version\`

---

## Performance Targets
- Backup creation: < 1s
- Verification: < 5s
- Rollback: < 1s
- Total update overhead: < 10s beyond download

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Backup created before every update
- [ ] Atomic replacement works (same filesystem)
- [ ] Cross-filesystem update works
- [ ] Post-update verification catches broken binary
- [ ] Automatic rollback on failure
- [ ] Manual rollback command works
- [ ] Backup cleanup keeps last 3 versions
- [ ] Unit tests pass with 95%+ coverage
- [ ] E2E tests pass including crash scenarios

 {"id":"process_triage-ica.1.3","title":"Implement homebrew-tap and scoop-bucket for package distribution","description":"## Overview
Create and maintain Homebrew tap and Scoop bucket for easy installation on macOS and Windows.

## Background
Package managers provide the easiest installation path for users. Homebrew (macOS/Linux) and Scoop (Windows) are the most popular options for CLI tools.

## Scope

### 1. Homebrew Tap
- Repository: \`process-triage/homebrew-tap\`
- Formula: \`pt.rb\`
  - Downloads binary from GitHub releases
  - Verifies SHA256 checksum
  - Optional: verify ECDSA signature
  - Dependencies: none (statically linked)
- Cask: Not needed (CLI tool)

**Formula Template:**
\`\`\`ruby
class Pt < Formula
  desc "Bayesian-inspired zombie/abandoned process detection"
  homepage "https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/process_triage"
  version "X.Y.Z"
  
  on_macos do
    on_arm do
      url "https://github.com/.../pt-core-darwin-aarch64.tar.gz"
      sha256 "..."
    end
    on_intel do
      url "https://github.com/.../pt-core-darwin-x86_64.tar.gz"
      sha256 "..."
    end
  end
  
  def install
    bin.install "pt-core"
    # Install shell completion, man pages
  end
  
  test do
    system "#{bin}/pt-core", "--version"
  end
end
\`\`\`

### 2. Scoop Bucket
- Repository: \`process-triage/scoop-bucket\`
- Manifest: \`pt.json\`
  - Downloads from GitHub releases
  - Extracts and adds to PATH
  - Checksum verification

**Manifest Template:**
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": "X.Y.Z",
  "description": "Bayesian zombie process detection",
  "homepage": "https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/process_triage",
  "license": "MIT",
  "architecture": {
    "64bit": {
      "url": "https://github.com/.../pt-core-windows-x86_64.zip",
      "hash": "..."
    }
  },
  "bin": "pt-core.exe",
  "checkver": "github",
  "autoupdate": { ... }
}
\`\`\`

### 3. Automation
- GitHub Action updates formulae/manifests on release
- Auto-PR to tap/bucket repos
- Version bumps automatic
- SHA256 hashes fetched from checksums.txt

### 4. Documentation
- Installation instructions in README
- Tap/bucket setup guide
- Troubleshooting common issues

---

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- **File**: \`scripts/package/test_formula.rb\` (Homebrew)
- **File**: \`scripts/package/test_manifest.ps1\` (Scoop)
- Test cases:
  - Formula syntax valid (ruby -c)
  - Manifest JSON valid
  - URLs resolve (HEAD request)
  - SHA256 hashes match

### Integration Tests
- **File**: \`.github/workflows/package-test.yml\`
- Test cases:
  - \`brew install process-triage/tap/pt\` succeeds on macOS
  - \`scoop install pt\` succeeds on Windows
  - Installed binary runs \`--version\` correctly
  - Upgrade works when new version available

### E2E Tests
- **File**: \`test/package_e2e.bats\`
- Test scenarios:
  - Fresh install on clean macOS (Docker)
  - Fresh install on clean Windows (GitHub runner)
  - Upgrade from previous version
  - Uninstall cleans up completely
- **Artifact logging**: Install logs, version output

### Release Automation Tests
- **File**: \`.github/workflows/release-package-test.yml\`
- Test cases:
  - Formula auto-update PR created
  - Manifest auto-update PR created
  - PRs have correct content

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events (for release automation)
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`package.formula_generate\` | INFO | version, sha256 | Formula generated |
| \`package.manifest_generate\` | INFO | version, sha256 | Manifest generated |
| \`package.pr_create\` | INFO | repo, pr_number | PR created |
| \`package.pr_merge\` | INFO | repo, pr_number | PR merged |

---

## Error Handling

### Failure Modes
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | Action |
|----------|-----------|----------|--------|
| Formula syntax error | ruby -c fails | Fix and re-run | Block release |
| SHA256 mismatch | Install fails | Re-fetch hash | Alert developer |
| GitHub unavailable | Download fails | Retry later | Cache last good |
| PR conflict | Merge fails | Manual resolution | Alert developer |

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Homebrew tap repository created
- [ ] \`brew install process-triage/tap/pt\` works
- [ ] Scoop bucket repository created
- [ ] \`scoop bucket add pt ...; scoop install pt\` works
- [ ] Auto-update on release works
- [ ] Installation tests pass on macOS
- [ ] Installation tests pass on Windows
- [ ] Documentation updated

 {"id":"process_triage-ica.1.4","title":"Implement musl static binary for portable Linux","description":"## Overview
Build statically-linked musl binaries for maximum Linux portability.

## Background
glibc-linked binaries fail on Alpine, older distros, and minimal containers. musl binaries run anywhere with a Linux kernel.

## Scope

### 1. Build Configuration
- Target: \`x86_64-unknown-linux-musl\`
- Target: \`aarch64-unknown-linux-musl\`
- Static linking: \`RUSTFLAGS="-C target-feature=+crt-static"\`
- Strip symbols: \`strip -s\` for smaller binary

### 2. CI Pipeline
- Use cross-rs for musl builds
- GitHub Actions matrix includes musl targets
- Build artifacts named: \`pt-core-linux-x86_64-musl\`, \`pt-core-linux-aarch64-musl\`

### 3. Verification
- Test on Alpine Linux (pure musl)
- Test on Ubuntu (glibc, should work)
- Test on Debian slim (minimal glibc)
- Test on BusyBox-based containers
- \`file\` command shows "statically linked"
- \`ldd\` shows "not a dynamic executable"

### 4. Size Optimization
- Enable LTO: \`lto = "fat"\`
- Strip symbols: \`strip = true\`
- Optimize for size: \`opt-level = "s"\` (or "z")
- Target size: < 15MB for core binary

### 5. Distribution
- Primary for Docker images
- Default for \`install.sh\` on Linux
- Fallback if glibc version mismatch

---

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- **File**: N/A (build infrastructure, no code changes)

### Integration Tests
- **File**: \`.github/workflows/musl-test.yml\`
- Test cases:
  - Build succeeds for x86_64-musl
  - Build succeeds for aarch64-musl
  - Binary is statically linked (ldd check)
  - Binary runs on Alpine
  - Binary runs on Ubuntu

### E2E Tests
- **File**: \`test/musl_e2e.bats\`
- Test scenarios (in Docker):
  - Run on \`alpine:latest\`: \`pt-core --version\` succeeds
  - Run on \`alpine:3.14\` (older): succeeds
  - Run on \`ubuntu:22.04\`: succeeds
  - Run on \`debian:slim\`: succeeds
  - Run on \`busybox:glibc\`: succeeds
  - Full workflow: scan, plan on Alpine
- **Artifact logging**: file/ldd output, test results

### Size Tests
- **File**: \`scripts/check_binary_size.sh\`
- Test cases:
  - Binary size < 15MB
  - Binary size doesn't regress by >10% vs previous release

### Compatibility Tests
- Matrix of distros and kernel versions
- Minimum kernel: 3.2 (for process scanning)
- Test on: Alpine 3.x, Debian 9+, Ubuntu 18.04+, CentOS 7+, Fedora 30+

---

## Logging Specifications

### Build Logging
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`build.musl_start\` | INFO | target, rust_version | Build starts |
| \`build.musl_complete\` | INFO | target, size_bytes, duration | Build done |
| \`build.static_check\` | INFO | is_static, linked_libs | Static verification |

---

## Error Handling

### Build Failures
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | Action |
|----------|-----------|----------|--------|
| musl-tools missing | rustup error | Install in CI | CI setup step |
| Link error | Build fails | Check dependencies | Review Cargo.toml |
| Size regression | Size check fails | Investigate deps | Profile binary |

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] x86_64-musl binary builds in CI
- [ ] aarch64-musl binary builds in CI
- [ ] Binary is truly statically linked
- [ ] Runs on Alpine Linux
- [ ] Runs on all tested distros
- [ ] Binary size < 15MB
- [ ] Published as part of releases

## Dependencies
- Part of: Advanced Installation epic (process_triage-ica.1)
- Requires: cross-rs for cross-compilation
- Requires: musl-tools or cross-rs containers","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T20:01:19.137863776Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T14:43:55.554187565Z","closed_at":"2026-01-17T14:43:55.554187565Z","close_reason":"Implemented musl static binary support: cargo/rustflags config for musl targets, CI workflows for building and testing musl binaries on multiple distros (Alpine, Ubuntu, Debian, CentOS, Fedora), binary size check script, BATS E2E tests for musl portability, and install.sh updates to auto-detect musl systems and use musl binaries as fallback.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-ica.1.4","depends_on_id":"process_triage-ica.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"process_triage-ica.2","title":"EPIC: Interactive Documentation and Tutorials","description":"## Overview
Create comprehensive documentation with interactive tutorials, video references, and example configurations for common setups.

## Background
Current documentation (README, AGENTS.md) is good but lacks interactive learning materials. Modern CLI tools benefit from rustlings-style tutorials and video walkthroughs.

## Research Findings
- Interactive CLI tutorials (rustlings pattern) accelerate learning
- Video walkthroughs help visual learners
- Example configurations reduce time-to-value
- Architecture docs help contributors

## Scope

### 1. Interactive Tutorial (\`pt learn\`)
- Guided exercises with verification
- Topics: 
  1. Basic scan (scanning first process list)
  2. Reading evidence (understanding the ledger)
  3. Understanding recommendations (classification scores)
  4. Applying plans (safe kill workflow)
  5. Robot mode basics (automation introduction)
  6. Shadow mode (calibration and learning)
  7. Deep scan (advanced evidence)
- Progress tracking (local state)
- Built into binary (no external dependency)

### 2. Example Configurations
- \`examples/configs/developer.json\`: Aggressive for dev machines
  - Lower thresholds, more frequent scans
  - Test runner patterns pre-configured
- \`examples/configs/server.json\`: Conservative for production
  - Higher thresholds, longer cooldowns
  - Database protection patterns
- \`examples/configs/ci.json\`: Headless CI/CD mode
  - No prompts, JSON output
  - Exit codes for automation
- \`examples/configs/fleet.json\`: Multi-host setup
  - Coordinator configuration
  - Node discovery settings
- Annotated with explanations in comments

### 3. Architecture Documentation
- ADR (Architecture Decision Records) directory
  - ADR-001: Four-state classification model
  - ADR-002: Bayesian inference approach
  - ADR-003: Safety gate design
  - ADR-004: FDR control mechanism
  - ADR-005: Telemetry lake architecture
- Component diagrams (Mermaid in docs)
- Data flow documentation
- Security model documentation

### 4. Video References
- README links to video walkthroughs
- Asciinema recordings for quick demos:
  - Basic scan workflow (30s)
  - Plan review and apply (60s)
  - Robot mode for agents (45s)
- YouTube playlist for longer tutorials

### 5. API Reference
- Generated from doc comments (cargo doc)
- Hosted on docs.rs
- JSON schema documentation with examples
- Exit code reference table
- Error message catalog

---

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/src/learn/tutorial_test.rs\`
- **Coverage target**: 85% for tutorial logic
- Test cases:
  - Each tutorial exercise validates correctly
  - Progress tracking state machine
  - Hint generation works
  - Reset clears progress

### Integration Tests
- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/tests/learn_integration.rs\`
- Test cases:
  - \`pt learn\` starts tutorial
  - Progress persists across invocations
  - \`pt learn --reset\` clears progress
  - Each exercise can complete

### E2E Tests
- **File**: \`test/docs_e2e.bats\`
- Test scenarios:
  - Example configs load without error
  - Example configs produce expected behavior
  - \`cargo doc\` generates without warnings
  - JSON schemas validate against examples
- **Artifact logging**: Generated docs, validation results

### Documentation Tests
- **File**: \`test/docs_validate.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - All code blocks in README are valid
  - All internal links resolve
  - All command examples work
  - ADRs follow template

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`learn.exercise_start\` | INFO | exercise_id, exercise_name | Tutorial progress |
| \`learn.exercise_complete\` | INFO | exercise_id, attempts, duration_ms | Completion tracking |
| \`learn.hint_shown\` | DEBUG | exercise_id, hint_number | Hint usage |
| \`learn.progress_save\` | DEBUG | total_complete, total_exercises | State persistence |

---

## Error Handling

### Failure Modes
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | User Message |
|----------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Progress file corrupt | JSON parse fails | Reset to beginning | "Progress file corrupted. Starting fresh." |
| Exercise verification fails | Check returns false | Show hint | "Not quite. Hint: ..." |
| Example config invalid | Schema validation | Skip with warning | "Example X has validation errors. Skipping." |

---

## Content Quality Standards
- All examples tested in CI
- Code blocks use language tags for syntax highlighting
- Screenshots/asciinema updated on major releases
- Documentation reviewed by non-author

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] \`pt learn\` command with 7+ exercises
- [ ] Example configs for 4 scenarios
- [ ] ADR directory with 5+ decisions
- [ ] Architecture diagram in docs
- [ ] At least 3 video/asciinema demos
- [ ] cargo doc generates clean docs
- [ ] All example configs validated in CI
- [ ] Documentation tests pass
- [ ] Logging meets specification above

 {"id":"process_triage-im2a","title":"Implement prior tuning from calibration data","description":"## Task: Prior Tuning from Calibration Data (Phase 9.3)

### Description
Automatically adjust prior distributions based on calibration data to improve model accuracy.

### Requirements
1. **Prior Parameters to Tune**
   - Process type priors (Beta α, β for each type)
   - Evidence weights (coefficient for each evidence source)
   - Threshold boundaries (KILL/REVIEW/SPARE cutoffs)
   - Expected lifetimes per process type

2. **Tuning Algorithm**
   ```
   For each prior parameter θ:
     1. Compute gradient of log-likelihood w.r.t. θ
     2. Update: θ_new = θ_old + η × ∇log(L)
     3. Regularize to prevent overfitting: θ_new = λ × θ_prior + (1-λ) × θ_new
     4. Validate on holdout set
     5. Accept if validation loss improves
   ```

3. **Tuning Output**
   ```json
   {
     "priors": {
       "test_runner": {"alpha": 3.2, "beta": 4.1, "expected_lifetime_hours": 0.75},
       "dev_server": {"alpha": 2.1, "beta": 5.8, "expected_lifetime_hours": 12},
       "agent_shell": {"alpha": 2.5, "beta": 6.2, "expected_lifetime_hours": 6}
     },
     "evidence_weights": {
       "orphaned": 38,
       "zero_cpu": 22,
       "high_memory": 12,
       "age_ratio": 1.8
     },
     "thresholds": {
       "kill": 62,
       "review": 28
     },
     "validation_metrics": {
       "brier_before": 0.15,
       "brier_after": 0.11,
       "improvement": "27%"
     }
   }
   ```

4. **Safety Constraints**
   - Maximum change per tuning round: ±20%
   - Minimum KILL threshold: 50
   - Maximum SPARE threshold: 40
   - Require N≥100 observations per type before tuning

### CLI Interface
```bash
# Preview tuning changes without applying
pt calibrate tune --dry-run

# Apply tuning with backup
pt calibrate tune --apply --backup

# Reset to default priors
pt calibrate reset
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tuning improves Brier score on validation set
- [ ] Safety constraints prevent extreme adjustments
- [ ] Tuning is reversible with backup
- [ ] Changes are logged for audit","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T09:03:43.146474378Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-02T05:19:50.445178276Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T05:19:50.445107735Z","close_reason":"Implemented TuningEngine with conservative prior adjustment from calibration data.  {"id":"process_triage-imji","title":"Implement visual session diff (side-by-side comparison)","description":"## Overview
Implement visual side-by-side comparison of two sessions, highlighting differences in candidates, classifications, and outcomes.

## Background
Users need to understand how their system changed between scans, or compare different configurations. Visual diff makes this easy.

## Scope

### 1. Diff Command
- \`pt diff <session1> <session2>\`: Compare two sessions
- \`pt diff --baseline\`: Compare current vs baseline
- \`pt diff --last\`: Compare current vs last session
- Output: Side-by-side view of differences

### 2. Diff Categories
**Processes:**
- New processes (in session2 but not session1)
- Gone processes (in session1 but not session2)
- Changed classification (same PID, different class)
- Changed score (significant score delta)

**Outcomes:**
- Killed in both / neither
- Killed in one only
- Classification changed but same outcome

**Evidence:**
- Evidence score changes
- New evidence sources
- Missing evidence sources

### 3. Output Formats
- **TUI**: Side-by-side panes with highlighting
- **JSON**: Structured diff for agents
- **Plain**: Text-based diff for terminals

### 4. Filtering
- \`--changed-only\`: Only show differences
- \`--category <type>\`: Filter by change type
- \`--min-score-delta N\`: Only show significant score changes

## Background
Modern coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Copilot) have configuration files. pt should auto-detect these and offer to configure itself as a tool.

### 1. Agent Detection
- Detect Claude Code: check for \`~/.claude/\` or \`claude\` in PATH
- Detect Codex: check for \`~/.codex/\` or \`codex\` in PATH  
- Detect Copilot: check for \`gh copilot\` extension
- Detect Cursor: check for \`~/.cursor/\` settings
- Detect Windsurf: check for \`~/.windsurf/\` or similar
- Detection order: most common first, fail fast

### 2. Configuration Generation
- Claude Code: Update \`~/.claude/settings.json\` with pt tool definition
  - Add pt to allowed tools list
  - Configure MCP server if desired
- Codex: Create MCP server configuration  
- Copilot: Suggest GitHub CLI alias setup
- Cursor: Update workspace settings with pt integration

### 3. Interactive Flow
- Show detected agents with versions
- Prompt for which to configure (multi-select)
- Preview changes before applying (diff-style)
- Backup existing configs before modification
- Confirm before each modification

### 4. Non-Interactive Mode
- \`pt agent init --yes\`: Apply defaults without prompts
- \`pt agent init --dry-run\`: Show what would change
- \`pt agent init --agent claude\`: Configure specific agent only
- \`pt agent init --skip-backup\`: Don't create backups (use with caution)

### 5. Output and Feedback
- Summary of changes made
- Instructions for verification
- Next steps documentation
- Rollback instructions if something goes wrong

 {"id":"process_triage-s8s.1.2","title":"Implement token-efficient output modes for agents","description":"## Overview
Implement token-efficient output modes for AI agent consumption, optimizing for limited context windows.

## Background
AI agents have limited context windows (Claude: 200k, GPT-4: 128k). Large pt outputs can consume significant tokens. Token-efficient modes let agents request minimal data.

## Scope

### 1. Field Selection
- \`--fields pid,score,classification\`: Select specific fields
- Predefined field sets:
  - \`--fields minimal\`: pid, classification only
  - \`--fields standard\`: pid, classification, score, command (default)
  - \`--fields full\`: all fields
- Custom field lists via comma separation

### 2. Compact Formats
- \`--format compact\`: Minimal JSON (no whitespace, short keys)
- \`--format lines\`: One JSON object per line (JSONL)
- \`--format csv\`: CSV for tabular consumers
- Key abbreviations: \`pid\` → \`p\`, \`classification\` → \`c\`, etc.

### 3. Token Estimation
- \`--max-tokens N\`: Hint for maximum output tokens
- Automatic truncation with continuation token
- Response includes: \`{ "truncated": true, "continuation": "TOKEN" }\`
- \`pt robot plan --continue TOKEN\`: Resume from truncation point

### 4. Delta Mode
- \`--delta\`: Only output changes since last call
- Session-based change tracking
- Useful for watch/poll patterns
- Response includes: \`{ "delta": true, "since": "TIMESTAMP" }\`

### 5. Response Size Preview
- \`--estimate-tokens\`: Return token estimate without full response
- Help agents decide whether to request full data
- Fast path: count without serialization overhead

 {"id":"process_triage-s8s.1.3","title":"Publish JSON schemas for all agent outputs","description":"## Overview
Publish JSON schemas for all agent-facing outputs to enable type-safe integrations.

## Background
AI agents and integrations benefit from type-safe APIs. JSON Schema provides machine-readable contracts for all pt outputs, enabling:
- Code generation for TypeScript/Python/Go clients
- Runtime validation of responses
- IDE autocomplete for consumers
- Documentation generation

## Scope

### 1. Schema Definition
- Define JSON Schema for all agent outputs:
  - \`ScanResult\`: Process list with evidence
  - \`Plan\`: Recommended actions
  - \`ApplyResult\`: Action outcomes
  - \`HistoryEntry\`: Past decisions
  - \`Candidate\`: Single process with evidence
  - \`EvidenceLedger\`: Full evidence breakdown
- Use JSON Schema Draft 2020-12

### 2. Schema Generation
- Auto-generate from Rust types using schemars
- Build-time schema generation (no runtime overhead)
- Version schemas with pt version
- Store in \`schemas/v1/\` directory

### 3. Schema Publishing
- Include schemas in binary (embedded)
- \`pt schema <type>\`: Print schema for type
- Publish to well-known URL: \`https://schemas.process-triage.dev/v1/<type>.json\`
- Host on GitHub Pages from \`schemas/\` directory

### 4. Type Generation
- Generate TypeScript types: \`npm run generate-types\`

Create a plugin architecture allowing users to add custom evidence sources and actions.

## Background
Different environments have different signals. A plugin system allows extending pt without forking:
- Custom metrics from application-specific sources
- Integration with internal monitoring systems
- Custom actions (restart via internal API, notify Slack)

## Scope

### 1. Plugin Architecture
- Plugin discovery via XDG directories (\`~/.config/process_triage/plugins/\`)
- Plugin manifest format (TOML with schema version)
- Versioned plugin API (v1 initially)
- Sandboxed execution (subprocess-based, no dynamic loading)
- Plugin lifecycle: init → collect/execute → cleanup

### 2. Evidence Source Plugins
- Interface: stdin receives PID list, stdout returns JSON evidence
- Standard schema for evidence output
- Examples:
  - \`plugin-prometheus\`: Fetch process metrics from Prometheus
  - \`plugin-datadog\`: Integration with Datadog agent
  - \`plugin-custom-db\`: Query internal database for process metadata
- Evidence weight configuration (how much to trust plugin data)

### 3. Action Plugins
- Interface: stdin receives action request, stdout returns result
- Standard schema for action request/response
- Examples:
  - \`plugin-slack\`: Notify Slack channel on kill
  - \`plugin-pagerduty\`: Create PagerDuty incident
  - \`plugin-kubernetes\`: Restart pod via kubectl
- Action safety: plugins can only suggest, pt executes

### 4. Plugin Development Kit
- Plugin template: \`pt plugin new <name>\`
- Testing utilities: \`pt plugin test <path>\`
- Documentation for plugin authors
- Example plugins in \`examples/plugins/\`

### 5. Plugin Security
- No dynamic library loading (subprocess only)
- Configurable timeouts per plugin
- Resource limits (memory, CPU)
- Manifest signing (optional, for trusted plugins)

- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/src/plugin/manager_test.rs\`

  - Action plugin receives request and returns result
  - Plugin errors don't crash main process
  - Timeout triggers clean shutdown of plugin
  - Multiple plugins run in parallel correctly

### E2E Tests
- **File**: \`test/plugin_e2e.bats\`

  - Install example plugin, run scan, verify evidence included
  - Plugin error: verify graceful degradation
  - Plugin timeout: verify scan completes without plugin data
  - \`pt plugin test\` validates example plugins

- **File**: \`crates/pt-core/tests/plugin_security.rs\`

  - Plugin cannot access files outside allowed paths
  - Plugin memory limit enforced
  - Plugin CPU limit enforced
  - Malicious plugin output rejected (oversized, malformed)

| \`plugin.error\` | WARN | name, error_message | Plugin error |

### Failure Modes
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | User Message |

| Plugin not found | File missing | Skip plugin | "Plugin X not found. Skipping." |
| Invalid manifest | TOML parse error | Skip plugin | "Plugin X has invalid manifest. Skipping." |

- Failed plugins don't fail the scan

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6","title":"EPIC: Phase 6 - Action Execution System","description":"## Overview
This epic covers the action execution system that safely applies decisions to processes.

## Background & Context
Section 6 specifies an action space beyond just kill:
- keep, pause, resume, renice, cgroup freeze, cgroup throttle
- cpuset quarantine, supervisor stop/restart
- zombie resolution (parent reaping)
- kill (SIGTERM → SIGKILL)

Actions must be:
- Safe (identity verified, privilege checked)
- Staged (less destructive first)
- Reversible (when possible)
- Auditable (fully logged)

## Why This Matters
- **Safety**: Wrong action on wrong process is catastrophic
- **Reversibility**: Prefer pause over kill when possible
- **Coordination**: Handle supervised processes correctly
- **Auditability**: Every action must be traceable

## Scope
1. Action plan generation (per-PID with ordering)
2. Identity revalidation before action
3. Staged execution protocol
4. Post-action verification
5. Failure recovery trees
6. Session/TTY protection
7. Lock coordination

## Success Criteria
- No PID reuse errors in testing
- Actions respect staging order
- Failures trigger recovery tree
- All actions logged with outcomes

## Technical Constraints
- Must revalidate (pid, start_id, uid) before action
- Default to same-UID only
- Acquire pt lock before action
- Protect current session

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A plan can be executed end-to-end with staged steps and post-action verification.
- [ ] Identity revalidation blocks stale/unsafe actions (PID reuse) deterministically.
- [ ] Zombie and D-state processes are handled via safe alternatives (no blind kill).
- [ ] Failure recovery trees are attached to actions and drive retries/fallbacks.
- [ ] All actions produce auditable outcome records (telemetry + human summary).

## Test Plan
- Unit: plan generation rules, identity checks, recovery tree selection.
- Integration: apply actions to controlled fixture processes (term/kill/respawn).
- E2E: golden-path workflow including after-action summary.

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6.1","title":"Implement zombie and D-state handling in action planning/execution","description":"## Purpose
Implement correct **zombie (`Z`)** and **uninterruptible sleep (`D`)** handling in action planning and execution.

These two OS states are special because “just kill it” is either impossible (zombie) or often ineffective/dangerous (D-state).

## Definitions (so future self doesn’t forget)
- **Zombie (`Z`)**: the process is already dead; it cannot be killed. The fix is to get the *parent* to reap it (or restart/kill the parent/supervisor).
- **Uninterruptible sleep (`D`)**: usually waiting on kernel I/O; may ignore SIGKILL. Blind killing can hang workflows and create false confidence.

## Required Behavior
### Zombie (`Z`)
- Detect zombies reliably.
- Do **not** propose `SIGTERM`/`SIGKILL` as the primary action for the zombie PID.
- Propose a safe alternative action plan, e.g.:
  - identify parent process
  - recommend supervisor-level action if parent is supervised
  - as a last resort, propose killing/restarting the parent (policy-gated)
- Ensure explainability:
  - evidence ledger / action rationale must clearly state “cannot kill a zombie; must address parent.”

### D-state (`D`)
- Detect D-state reliably.
- Default recommendation should be **investigate/mitigate**, not “kill harder.”
- Surface diagnostics (as evidence/provenance), e.g.:
  - `wchan` (what it’s blocked on)
  - relevant I/O counters deltas
- If any destructive action is proposed, it must be:
  - explicitly policy-gated
  - explained as low-probability-of-success
  - accompanied by suggested next steps (restart supervisor, check mount, etc.)

## Interaction With Decision Theory
- Expected-loss computation must treat Z/D specially:
  - Z: action feasibility constraint (kill action invalid)
  - D: action success probability low; increased uncertainty/robust gating

## Logging / Telemetry Requirements
- Always log:
  - detected state (`Z`/`D`)
  - chosen action routing (parent/supervisor/investigate)
  - diagnostics gathered (wchan, etc.)
- Ensure logs are useful for postmortems (why we didn’t kill / why we suggested restart).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Zombie targets never receive a direct kill action in the generated plan.
- [ ] Zombie handling routes to a parent/supervisor-aware action (or explicit “investigate only”) with clear rationale.
- [ ] D-state targets default to investigate/mitigate and surface wchan/diagnostics.
- [ ] Any kill-like action for D-state is policy-gated and clearly labeled “low confidence / may not work.”
- [ ] Structured logs/telemetry include state classification and routing decisions.

## Test Plan
- Unit:
  - planner routing logic for Z vs non-Z
